subject: Chemistry
q:
$\mathrm{A}+2 \mathrm{~B} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AB}_{2}$
36.0 g of 'A' (Molar mass: $60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$) and 56.0 g of ' $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ (Molar mass: $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$) are allowed to react. Which of the following statements are correct ?
A. 'A' is the limiting reagent.
B. $77.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{AB}_{2}$ is formed.
C. Molar mass of $A B_{2}$ is $140 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
D. $15.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of A is left unreacted after the completion of reaction.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
$\mathrm{A}+2 \mathrm{~B} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AB}_{2}$
36.0 g of 'A' (Molar mass: $60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$) and 56.0 g of ' $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ (Molar mass: $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$) are allowed to react. Which of the following statements are correct ?
A. 'A' is the limiting reagent.
B. $77.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{AB}_{2}$ is formed.
C. Molar mass of $A B_{2}$ is $140 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
D. $15.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of A is left unreacted after the completion of reaction.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Reaction: A + 2B → AB₂.
Moles of A = 36.0/60 = 0.6 mol;
Moles of B = 56.0/80 = 0.7 mol.
A requires 1.2 mol of B but only 0.7 available, so B is limiting reagent.
So, (A) False - B is limiting.
(B) AB₂ formed = 0.7/2 = 0.35 mol;
Molar mass AB₂ = 60 + 2(80) = 220 g/mol;
Mass = 0.35 × 220 = 77 g True.
(C) False - Molar mass is 220 g/mol.
(D) A remaining = 0.6 - 0.35 = 0.25 mol = 15 g True.
Correct statements: B and D.

subject: Chemistry
q: How many moles of magnesium phosphate, $\mathrm{Mg}_3\left(\mathrm{PO}_4\right)_2$ will contain $0.25$ mole of oxygen atoms?
question: How many moles of magnesium phosphate, $\mathrm{Mg}_3\left(\mathrm{PO}_4\right)_2$ will contain $0.25$ mole of oxygen atoms?
solution:
$\mathrm{Mg}_3\left(\mathrm{PO}_4\right)_2$
'n' moles
$8 \mathrm{n}=0.25$
$\mathrm{n}=\frac{0.25}{8}$
$\quad=\frac{25}{8 \times 100}=3.125 \times 10^{-2}$

subject: Chemistry
q: A solution is prepared by adding <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mo>'</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>''</mo></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> mole of water. Mass percent of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>''</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>''</mo></math>in solution is
question: A solution is prepared by adding <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mo>'</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>''</mo></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> mole of water. Mass percent of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>''</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>''</mo></math>in solution is
solution:
To calculate the mass percent of ''X'' in the solution, we need to determine the mass of ''X'' and the total mass of the solution.

Given:

:Solute (X) = 2 g

Solvent (H2O) = 1 mole = 18 g

Total mass = 2 + 18 = 20 g

 Mass percent of X=22+18×100=10

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-I
Mass of substance		List-II
Number of atoms
A. $1.8$ mg waterI. $2\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
B. $9.8$ mg sulphuric acidII. $1.5\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
C. $1.8$ mg carbonIII. $3\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
D. $5.85$ mg salt (NaCl)IV. $7\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-I
Mass of substance		List-II
Number of atoms
A. $1.8$ mg waterI. $2\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
B. $9.8$ mg sulphuric acidII. $1.5\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
C. $1.8$ mg carbonIII. $3\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
D. $5.85$ mg salt (NaCl)IV. $7\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
For A: Molar mass of $H_2O = 18$ g/mol. Moles of $H_2O = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{18} = 10^{-4}$. Since each $H_2O$ molecule has $3$ atoms, the number of atoms = $3 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

For B: Molar mass of $H_2SO_4 = 98$ g/mol. Moles of $H_2SO_4 = \dfrac{9.8 \times 10^{-3}}{98} = 10^{-4}$. Since each $H_2SO_4$ molecule has $7$ atoms, the number of atoms = $7 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

For C: Molar mass of $C = 12$ g/mol. Moles of $C = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$. The number of atoms = $1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

For D: Molar mass of $NaCl = 58.5$ g/mol. Moles of $NaCl = \dfrac{5.85 \times 10^{-3}}{58.5} = 10^{-4}$. Since each $NaCl$ formula unit has $2$ atoms, the number of atoms = $2 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

Thus, the correct matching is A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I.

Answer: A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I

subject: Chemistry
q:
Zinc reacts with hydrochloric acid to give hydrogen and zinc chloride. The volume of hydrogen gas produced at STP from the reaction of 11.5 g of zinc with excess HCl is L (Nearest integer)
(Given : Molar mass of Zn is 65.4 g mol-1 and Molar volume of H2 at STP=22.7 L )
question:
Zinc reacts with hydrochloric acid to give hydrogen and zinc chloride. The volume of hydrogen gas produced at STP from the reaction of 11.5 g of zinc with excess HCl is L (Nearest integer)
(Given : Molar mass of Zn is 65.4 g mol-1 and Molar volume of H2 at STP=22.7 L )
solution:
Zinc reacts with hydrochloric acid to give hydrogen and zinc chloride.

Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl2 + H2↑

The molar mass of Zn is 65.4 g mol-1

Molar volume od H2 at STP = 22.7 L

Number of moles of Zn =11.565.4= Number of moles of  H2evolved 

Volume of H2 = 11.565.4 × 22.7 L = 3.99 L ≈ 4 L

subject: Chemistry
q: A gaseous compound of nitrogen and hydrogen contains $12.5 \%$ (by mass) of hydrogen. The density of the compound relative to hydrogen is 16. The molecular formula of the compound is:
question: A gaseous compound of nitrogen and hydrogen contains $12.5 \%$ (by mass) of hydrogen. The density of the compound relative to hydrogen is 16. The molecular formula of the compound is:
solution:
In an unknown compounds containing $\mathrm{N}$ and $\mathrm{H}$
given $\%$ of $\mathrm{H}=12.5 \%$
$$
\therefore \% \text { of } N=100-12.5=87.5 \%
$$

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline \text { Element } & \text { Percentage } & \text { Atomic ratio } & \text { Simple ratio } \\
\hline \mathrm{H} & 12.5 \% & \frac{12.5}{1}=12.5 & \frac{12.5}{6.25}=2 \\
\hline \mathrm{N} & 87.5 & \frac{87.5}{14}=6.25 & \frac{6.25}{6.25}=1 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

$2 \times$ vapour density $=$ Mol. wt $=16 \times 2=32$.
Molecular formula $=n \times$ empirical formula mass
$$
n=\frac{32}{16}=2
$$
$\therefore$ Molecular formula of the compound will be $=\left(\mathrm{NH}_2\right)_2$ $=\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{H}_4$

subject: Chemistry
q: Molarity \((\mathrm{M})\) of an aqueous solution containing \(x \mathrm{~g}\) of anhyd. \(\mathrm{CuSO}_4\) in \(500 \mathrm{~mL}\) solution at \(32{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) is \(2 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{M}\). Its molality will be ______ \(\times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\). (nearest integer). [Given density of the solution \(=1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}\)]
question: Molarity \((\mathrm{M})\) of an aqueous solution containing \(x \mathrm{~g}\) of anhyd. \(\mathrm{CuSO}_4\) in \(500 \mathrm{~mL}\) solution at \(32{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) is \(2 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{M}\). Its molality will be ______ \(\times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\). (nearest integer). [Given density of the solution \(=1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}\)]
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{M}_{\text {sol }^{\mathrm{I}}}=\mathrm{v}_{\text {sol }^{\mathrm{n}}} \times \mathrm{d}_{\text {sol }^{\mathrm{n}}} \\ &=500 \times 1.25=625 \mathrm{~g} \\ & \text { Mass of solute }(\mathrm{x})=0.2 \times 0.5 \times 159.5 \\ & \quad=15.95\end{aligned} \\ & \mathrm{n}_{\text {solute }}=0.1, \quad \\ & \text { Mass of solvent = Mass of solution }- \text { Mass of solute } \\ & =625-15.95 \\ & =609.05 \\ & \mathrm{~m}=\frac{0.1}{\frac{609.05}{1000}} \\ & \mathrm{~m}=0.164=164 \times 10^{-3}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The most abundant elements by mass in the body of a healthy human adult are:

Oxygen (61.4%); Carbon (22.9%), Hydrogen (10.0%); and Nitrogen (2.6%). The weight which a 75kg person would gain if all  1H atoms are replaced by  2H atoms is:
question:
The most abundant elements by mass in the body of a healthy human adult are:

Oxygen (61.4%); Carbon (22.9%), Hydrogen (10.0%); and Nitrogen (2.6%). The weight which a 75kg person would gain if all  1H atoms are replaced by  2H atoms is:
solution:
Weight of Hydrogen in body of 75 kg person =75×10100=7.5kg

On replacing  1H by  2H , the new weight of H=7.5×2=15kg

Weight increase = 7.5kg

subject: Chemistry
q: If 5 moles of BaCl2 is mixed with 2 moles of Na3PO4, the maximum number of moles of Ba3PO42formed is _________ (Nearest integer)
question: If 5 moles of BaCl2 is mixed with 2 moles of Na3PO4, the maximum number of moles of Ba3PO42formed is _________ (Nearest integer)
solution:
The balanced chemical equation for the reaction between BaCl2 and Na3PO4 is:

3BaCl2 + 2Na3PO4 → Ba3(PO4)2 + 6NaCl

5 moles of barium chloride will require 5×23=3.3 moles of sodium phosphate.

Here, sodium phosphate is the limiting reagent.
2 moles of sodium phosphate produce 1 mole of barium phosphate.

Thus,2 moles of sodium phosphate will produce 1 mole of barium phosphate.

Hence, the maximum no. of moles of Ba3(PO4)2 that can be formed is one mole.

subject: Chemistry
q: The volume of hydrogen liberated at STP by treating 2.4 g of magnesium with excess of hydrochloric acid is ......×10-2 L. Given Molar volume of gas is 22.4 L at STP. Molar mass of magnesium is 24 g mol-1
question: The volume of hydrogen liberated at STP by treating 2.4 g of magnesium with excess of hydrochloric acid is ......×10-2 L. Given Molar volume of gas is 22.4 L at STP. Molar mass of magnesium is 24 g mol-1
solution:
The stoichiometric equation of the reaction between magnesium and hydrogen chloride can be written as follows,

One mole magnesium can liberate one mole of hydrogen gas according to the following equation.

 Mg2.4 g=0.1 mole+2HCl→MgCl2+H20.1 mole
Volume of 1 mole H2 at =22.4 L 
∴ 0.1 mole H2 at STP will occupy =2.24 L

Number of moles=Mass / Molar mass

nAcetanilide =nAniline 

⇒m135=9.393

subject: Chemistry
q:
Aqueous HCl reacts with $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})$ to form $\mathrm{MnCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}), \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)$. What is the weight (in g) of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ liberated when 8.7 g of $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})$ is reacted with excess aqueous HCl solution?
(Given Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{Mn}=55, \mathrm{Cl}=35.5, \mathrm{O}=16, \mathrm{H}=1$)
question:
Aqueous HCl reacts with $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})$ to form $\mathrm{MnCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}), \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)$. What is the weight (in g) of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ liberated when 8.7 g of $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})$ is reacted with excess aqueous HCl solution?
(Given Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{Mn}=55, \mathrm{Cl}=35.5, \mathrm{O}=16, \mathrm{H}=1$)
solution:
The balanced redox equation is: $\text{MnO}_2 + 4\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MnCl}_2 + \text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
Molar mass of MnO₂ = 55 + 32 = 87 g/mol.
Moles of MnO₂ = $\frac{8.7}{87} = 0.1$ mol.
From stoichiometry, 1 mol MnO₂ produces 1 mol Cl₂, so 0.1 mol produces 0.1 mol Cl₂.
Mass of Cl₂ = $0.1 \times 71 = 7.1$ g.

subject: Chemistry
q: The hydrocarbon $(X)$ with molar mass $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $90 \%$ carbon has $\ldots\ldots$ degree of unsaturation.
question: The hydrocarbon $(X)$ with molar mass $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $90 \%$ carbon has $\ldots\ldots$ degree of unsaturation.
solution: $\therefore$ Empirical formula is $\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_4$ Molecular mass $=80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Molecular formula $=\left(\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_4\right)_{\mathrm{n}}$ $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\mathrm{EF} \text { mass }}=\frac{80}{40}=2$ $\therefore$ Molecular formula is $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_8$ Degree of unsaturation $=\frac{2 \times 6+2-8}{2}=3$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The correct order of total number of atoms present in
(A) $2$ moles of cyclohexane
(B) $684$ g of sucrose
(C) $90.8$ L of dihydrogen at STP
is:
question:
The correct order of total number of atoms present in
(A) $2$ moles of cyclohexane
(B) $684$ g of sucrose
(C) $90.8$ L of dihydrogen at STP
is:
solution:
For (A):

Formula of cyclohexane is $C_6H_{12}$.

Number of atoms per molecule = $6 + 12 = 18$.

Total number of atoms in $2$ moles = $2 \times 18 \times N_A = 36 N_A$.

For (B):

Formula of sucrose is $C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}$.

Molar mass of sucrose = $342$ g/mol.

Number of moles = $\dfrac{684}{342} = 2$ moles.

Number of atoms per molecule = $12 + 22 + 11 = 45$.

Total number of atoms = $2 \times 45 \times N_A = 90 N_A$.

For (C):

Formula of dihydrogen is $H_2$.

Number of moles at STP = $\dfrac{90.8}{22.7} = 4$ moles.

Number of atoms per molecule = $2$.

Total number of atoms = $4 \times 2 \times N_A = 8 N_A$.

Comparing the total number of atoms:

$90 N_A > 36 N_A > 8 N_A$

Therefore, the correct order is $B > A > C$.

Answer: $B>A>C$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The density of 3M solution of NaCl is 1.0 g mL-1. Molality of the solution is ______×10-2 m (Nearest integer).

Given: Molar mass of Na and Cl is 23 and 35.5 g mol-1 respectively.
question:
The density of 3M solution of NaCl is 1.0 g mL-1. Molality of the solution is ______×10-2 m (Nearest integer).

Given: Molar mass of Na and Cl is 23 and 35.5 g mol-1 respectively.
solution:
Formula unit mass of sodium chloride = 23 + 35.5 gmol-1 = 58.5 gmol-1
∴m(molality)=1000×M1000×d-M×M.W of solute    ...(M = Molarity of solution)

=1000×31000×1-(3×58.5)=3.64

=364×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q: Number of hydrogen atoms per molecule of a hydrocarbon A having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>85</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>%</mo></math> carbon is (Given : Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>84</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
question: Number of hydrogen atoms per molecule of a hydrocarbon A having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>85</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>%</mo></math> carbon is (Given : Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>84</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
solution:
Using table of empirical formula for the compound:
Element Percentage Mole ratio
C 85.8 85.812=7.15 1
H 14.2 14.21=14.2 2

 

Thus, empirical formula is CH2, empirical molecular mass = 14
Thus, Molecular MassEmpirical Mass = n

14×n=84

n=6

∴ Molecular formula C6H12

subject: Chemistry
q: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>35</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> lead nitrate solution is mixed with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> chromic sulphate solution, ____________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math> moles of lead sulphate precipitate out. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
question: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>35</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> lead nitrate solution is mixed with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> chromic sulphate solution, ____________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math> moles of lead sulphate precipitate out. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
solution:
3 PbNO32+Cr2SO43→3PbSO4+2CrNO33

  35 ml        20 ml0.15 M0.12 M

=5.25 m.mol=2.4 m.mol5.25 m.mol

=5.25×10-3 mol

Therefore moles of PbSO4 formed

=5.25×10-3 =525×10-5

subject: Chemistry
q:
In the following reaction:

A+2B+3C⇌AB2C3
6.0 g of A, 6.0×1023  atoms of B and 0.036 mol of C reacted and formed 4.8 g of compound AB2C3. If the atomic mass of A  and C are 60 and 80 amu, respectively. What is the atomic mass of B in amu? (Avogadro number=6×1023)
question:
In the following reaction:

A+2B+3C⇌AB2C3
6.0 g of A, 6.0×1023  atoms of B and 0.036 mol of C reacted and formed 4.8 g of compound AB2C3. If the atomic mass of A  and C are 60 and 80 amu, respectively. What is the atomic mass of B in amu? (Avogadro number=6×1023)
solution:
A + 2 B + 3 C → AB 2 C 3 6 g 6 × 1 0 2 3 atoms 0.036 moles Moles 6 6 0 6 × 1 0 2 3 6 × 1 0 2 3 0.1 mole 1.0 mole 0.036 moles

0.11 ,1.02 ,0.0363; the least value is 0.012, i.e., C is the limiting reagent
0.036 moles of C provides 0.012 moles of AB2C3.

0.012 moles of AB2C3 weighs 4.8 g
1 mole of AB2C3 weighs 4.80.012=400 g mol-1
400=60+2B+3×80
B=50 amu

question: An aqueous solution of oxalic acid dihydrate contains its $6.3 \mathrm{~g}$ in $250 \mathrm{ml}$. The volume of $0.1 \mathrm{~N}$ $\mathrm{NaOH}$ required to completely neutralize $10 \mathrm{ml}$ of this solution

subject: Chemistry
q: A gaseous hydrocarbon on combustion gives 0.72 g of water and 3.08 g CO2. What is the empirical formula of the hydrocarbon?
question: A gaseous hydrocarbon on combustion gives 0.72 g of water and 3.08 g CO2. What is the empirical formula of the hydrocarbon?
solution:
C x H y + x + y 4 O 2 ⟶ xC O 2 + y 2 H 2 O

Moles of CO2 = 3 . 0 8 4 4 = 0 . 0 7  moles = x

Moles of H2O = 0 . 7 2 1 8 = 0 . 0 4  moles = y 2

x = 0.07 g atoms of carbon

y = 0.08 g atoms of hydrogen

   x       :        y0.07        0.08   7              8

Empirical formula becomes C7H8.

subject: Chemistry
q: To neutralize completely $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ aqueous solution of phosphorous acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_3\right)$, the volume of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ aqueous $\mathrm{KOH}$ solution required is
question: To neutralize completely $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ aqueous solution of phosphorous acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_3\right)$, the volume of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ aqueous $\mathrm{KOH}$ solution required is
solution:
No. of gm equivalents of phosphorous acid $=\mathrm{No}$. of gm equivalents of $\mathrm{KOH}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 20 \times 0.1 \times 2(n=\text { factor })=0.1 \times V \\
& =0.1 \times V
\end{aligned}
$$ 
$\mathrm{V}=\frac{4}{0.1}=40 \mathrm{ml}$

q: The number of significant figures in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50000</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>020</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is <u>                  </u>
question: The number of significant figures in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50000</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>020</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is <u>                  </u>
solution: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><menclose notation="bottom"><mn>50000</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn></menclose><mn>0</mn></menclose><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> All digits are significant.

subject: Chemistry
q: At <math><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> and <math><mn>1</mn></math> atmospheric pressure, <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of a hydrocarbon required <math><mn>55</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> for complete combustion, and <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is formed. The formula of the hydrocarbon is:
question: At <math><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> and <math><mn>1</mn></math> atmospheric pressure, <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of a hydrocarbon required <math><mn>55</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> for complete combustion, and <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is formed. The formula of the hydrocarbon is:
solution:
Let hydrocarbon is CxHy

10x=40 ; x=4 and 10x+y4=55
x+y4=5510
x+y4=5.5
Put the value of x
4+y4=5.5
y4=5.5-4
y4=1.5 then y=6
Hence hydrocarbon is C4H6

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> on reaction with excess of water results into acidic mixture <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>→</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>HCl</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> is required for the complete neutralisation of the resultant acidic mixture. The number of moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> used is
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> on reaction with excess of water results into acidic mixture <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>→</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>HCl</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> is required for the complete neutralisation of the resultant acidic mixture. The number of moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> used is
solution:
SO2Cl2+2H2O→2HCl+H2SO4

Let x moles of SO2Cl2 are required,

∴ nH2SO4=x,nHCl=2x

⇒nH+=4x

Moles of H+ formed =4x

Moles of OH- from 16 moles of NaOH=16

4x=16 (for complete neutralisation)

x=4

subject: Chemistry
q: What volume of hydrogen gas, at $273 \mathrm{~K}$ and $1 \mathrm{~atm}$, pressure will be consumed in obtaining $21.6 \mathrm{~g}$ of elemental boron (atomic mass $=10.8$ ) from the reducti on of boron trichloride by hydrogen?
question: What volume of hydrogen gas, at $273 \mathrm{~K}$ and $1 \mathrm{~atm}$, pressure will be consumed in obtaining $21.6 \mathrm{~g}$ of elemental boron (atomic mass $=10.8$ ) from the reducti on of boron trichloride by hydrogen?
solution:
No. of moles of boron $=\frac{21.6}{10.8}=2$
for $\mathrm{BCl}_3$
$\therefore 1$ mole of Boron $=3$ mole of $\mathrm{Cl}$
$\therefore 2$ mole of Boron $=6$ mole of $\mathrm{Cl}$
$\mathrm{H}_2+\mathrm{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCI}$
$\Rightarrow 3$ moles of Hydrogen is required
$=3 \times 22.4=67.2$ Litre

subject: Chemistry
q: The moles of methane required to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>81</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water after complete combustion is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></math>. [nearest integer]
question: The moles of methane required to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>81</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water after complete combustion is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></math>. [nearest integer]
solution:
CH4+2O2⟶CO2+2H2O

POAC on H atom

nCH4×4=nH2O×2

nCH4=8118×2×14=8136

nCH4=2.25

=225×10-2

Hence, the number of moles of methane is 225×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q: Number of atoms in $558.5$ gram Fe (at.wt. of $\mathrm{Fe}=55.85 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) is
question: Number of atoms in $558.5$ gram Fe (at.wt. of $\mathrm{Fe}=55.85 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) is
solution:
$\mathrm{Fe}($ no. of moles $)=\frac{558.5}{55.85}=10$ moles
$C$ (no. of moles $)=60 / 12=5$ moles.

subject: Chemistry
q: Density of 3 M NaCl solution is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. The molality of the solution is :
question: Density of 3 M NaCl solution is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. The molality of the solution is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 3 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaCl}, \mathrm{d}_{\text {sol }}=1.25 \mathrm{gm} / \mathrm{mol} \\ & \begin{aligned} \text { Molality } & =\frac{\mathrm{M} \times 1000}{1000 \mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{M} \times \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{W}}} \\ & =\frac{3000}{1250-175.5}=2.79\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Xg of benzoic acid on reaction with aq $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$ released $\mathrm{CO}_2$ that occupied 11.2 L volume at STP.
X is _____ g.
question:
Xg of benzoic acid on reaction with aq $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$ released $\mathrm{CO}_2$ that occupied 11.2 L volume at STP.
X is _____ g.
solution: 1 mole benzoic acid will release 1 mole $\mathrm{CO}_2$ 11.2 $\mathrm{L} \mathrm{CO}_2$ at STP is $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~mole}_{\mathrm{CO}}^2$ which will be released by reaction with $\frac{1}{2}$ mole benzoic acid $\begin{aligned} \text { Mass of benzoic acid } & =\frac{1}{2} \times 122 \\ & =61 \mathrm{gm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of silver nitrate solution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is added dropwise to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of potassium iodide <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>)</mo></math> solution. The ion(s) present in very small quantity in the solution is/are
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of silver nitrate solution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is added dropwise to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of potassium iodide <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>)</mo></math> solution. The ion(s) present in very small quantity in the solution is/are
solution:
To determine the ion(s) present in very small quantity in the solution, we need to consider the chemical reaction that occurs when silver nitrate reacts with potassium iodide. The reaction between AgNO3 and KI is a double replacement reaction and can be represented as follows:

AgNO3 + KI → AgI + KNO3

Millimoles of AgNO3 = 25

Millimoles of KI = 25 × 1.05=26.25

∴KI is in excess & AgI forms negatively charged colloid. (Some Ag+remains in solution) lons Ag+& F-are therefore, present in very small quantity.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The amount of calcium oxide produced on heating 150 kg limestone ( $75 \%$ pure) is ______ kg. (Nearest integer)
Given : Molar mass (in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$) of Ca-40, O-16, C-12
question:
The amount of calcium oxide produced on heating 150 kg limestone ( $75 \%$ pure) is ______ kg. (Nearest integer)
Given : Molar mass (in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$) of Ca-40, O-16, C-12
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{CO}_2 \\ & \text { mass of } \mathrm{CaCO}_3=\frac{150 \times 75}{100}=112.5 \mathrm{~kg} \\ & =112500 \mathrm{~g} \\ & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CaCO}_3}=1125 \\ & \mathrm{So} \text { moles of } \mathrm{CaO}=1125 \\ & \quad \text { mass of } \mathrm{CaO}=\frac{1125 \times 56}{1000}=63 \mathrm{~kg} \\ & \text { Correct answer } \Rightarrow 63\end{aligned}$
